{{Short description|Exclusion of someone from social relations}}
[[File:Admonitions Scroll Scene 10.jpg|300px|thumb|alt=A woman walking towards a man who has raised his hand towards her and is turning away|This scene of the ''[[Admonitions Scroll]]'' shows an emperor turning away from his consort, his hand raised in a gesture of rejection and with a look of disdain on his face.<ref>{{Citation | last=McCausland | first=Shane | year=2003 | title=First Masterpiece of Chinese Painting: The Admonitions Scroll | publisher=[[British Museum Press]] | page=75 | isbn=978-0-7141-2417-9 }}</ref>]]
{{Community}}
{{Emotion}}
'''Social rejection''' occurs when an individual is deliberately or accidentally excluded from a [[interpersonal relationship|social relationship]] or [[social interaction]]. The topic includes ''interpersonal rejection'' (or peer rejection), ''romantic rejection'', and ''familial estrangement''. A person can be rejected or [[shunned]] by individuals or an entire group of people. Furthermore, rejection can be either ''active'' by [[bullying]], [[teasing]], or ridiculing, or ''[[passive-aggressive behaviour|passive]]'' by ignoring a person, or giving the "[[silent treatment]]". The experience of being rejected is [[subjectivity|subjective]] for the recipient, and it can be [[perception|perceived]] when it is not actually present. The word "[[ostracism]]" is also commonly used to denote a process of social exclusion (in [[Ancient Greece]], ''ostracism'' was a form of temporary [[banishment]] following a people's vote).<ref>{{cite encyclopedia |encyclopedia=Dictionary.com |title=ostracize |url=http://dictionary.reference.com/browse/ostracize |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211022225746/https://www.dictionary.com/browse/ostracize |archive-date=22 October 2021 |url-status=live}}</ref>

Although [[humans]] are [[society|social]] beings, some level of rejection is an inevitable part of life. Nevertheless, rejection can become a problem when it is prolonged or consistent, when the relationship is important, or when the individual is highly sensitive to rejection. Rejection by an entire [[group (sociology)|group]] of people can have especially negative effects, particularly when it results in [[social isolation]].<ref>{{cite book |last=Williams |first=Kipling D. |url=https://archive.org/details/socialoutcastost0000unse/page/366/mode/2up |title=The Social Outcast: Ostracism, Social Exclusion, Rejection, and Bullying |author2=Joseph P. Forgas |author3=William von Hippel |publisher=Psychology Press |year=2005 |isbn=978-1-84169-424-5 |page=366 pages |url-access=registration}}</ref>

The experience of rejection can lead to a number of adverse psychological consequences such as [[loneliness]], low [[self-esteem]], [[aggression]], [[clinical depression|depression]], and [[nightmare disorder|nightmares]].<ref name="McDougall, P. 2001 pp. 213-247">McDougall, P., Hymel, S., Vaillancourt, T., & Mercer, L. (2001). The consequences of childhood rejection. In M. R. Leary (Ed.), ''Interpersonal rejection.'' (pp. 213–247). New York, NY: Oxford University Press.</ref> It can also lead to feelings of [[Emotional insecurity|insecurity]] and a heightened sensitivity to future rejection.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Richman|first1=Laura Smart|last2=Leary|first2=Mark R.|date=April 2009|title=Reactions to Discrimination, Stigmatization, Ostracism, and Other Forms of Interpersonal Rejection|journal=Psychological Review|volume=116|issue=2|pages=365–383|doi=10.1037/a0015250|issn=0033-295X|pmc=2763620|pmid=19348546|hdl=10161/11810}}</ref>

Some perspectives in popular psychology suggest that early or repeated experiences of social rejection may contribute to long-term emotional patterns linked to anxiety. Frameworks such as the "five emotional wounds" model describe a "rejection wound," in which individuals may develop heightened sensitivity to criticism, avoidance of vulnerability, or chronic fear of exclusion.<ref>{{cite web |title=The 5 Emotional Wounds by Lise Bourbeau & How They Fuel Anxiety |url=https://anxietychecklist.com/5-emotional-wounds-anxiety |access-date=2025-12-13 |website=anxietychecklist.com}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2024-12-04 |title=Lessons From The 5 Soul Wounds |url=https://www.brainzmagazine.com/post/lessons-from-the-5-soul-wounds |access-date=2025-12-13 |website=Brainz Magazine |language=en}}</ref> 

{{Censorship sidebar}}

== Need for acceptance ==
Social rejection may be emotionally painful, due to the social nature of human beings, as well as the essential need for social interaction between other humans. [[Abraham Maslow]] and other theorists have suggested that the need for love and [[belongingness]] is a fundamental human [[motivation]].<ref>Maslow, A. H. (1954). ''Motivation and personality''. New York, NY: Harper.</ref> According to Maslow, all humans, even [[introvert]]s, need to be able to give and receive affection to be psychologically healthy.

Psychologists believe that simple contact or social interaction with others is not enough to fulfill this need. Instead, people have a strong motivational drive to form and maintain caring interpersonal relationships. People need both stable relationships and satisfying interactions with the people in those relationships. If either of these two ingredients is missing, people will begin to feel lonely and unhappy.<ref name="pmid7777651">{{cite journal|vauthors=Baumeister RF, Leary MR | title=The need to belong: desire for interpersonal attachments as a fundamental human motivation | journal=Psychol Bull | year= 1995 | volume= 117 | issue= 3 | pages= 497–529 | pmid=7777651 | doi= 10.1037/0033-2909.117.3.497| s2cid=13559932 }}</ref> Thus, rejection is a significant threat. In fact, the majority of human [[anxiety|anxieties]] appear to reflect concerns over [[social exclusion]].<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Baumeister | first1 = R. F. | last2 = Tice | first2 = D. M. | year = 1990 | title = Anxiety and social exclusion | journal = Journal of Social and Clinical Psychology | volume = 9 | issue = 2| pages = 165–195 | doi=10.1521/jscp.1990.9.2.165}}</ref>

Being a member of a group is also important for [[social identity]], which is a key component of the [[self-concept]]. Mark Leary of Duke University has suggested that the main purpose of [[self-esteem]] is to monitor social relations and detect social rejection. In this view, self-esteem is a [[sociometer]] which activates negative emotions when signs of exclusion appear.<ref>Leary, M. R., Downs, D. L., (1995). Interpersonal functions of the self-esteem motive: The self-esteem system as a sociometer. In M. H. Kernis, ''Efficacy, agency, and self-esteem''. New York, NY: Plenum Press, pp. 123-144.</ref>

[[Social psychology (psychology)|Social psychological]] research confirms the motivational basis of the need for acceptance. Specifically, fear of rejection leads to [[conformity (psychology)|conformity]] to [[peer pressure]] (sometimes called normative influence, cf. informational influence), and [[social psychology (psychology)|compliance]] to the demands of others. The need for affiliation and social interaction appears to be particularly strong under stress.<ref>Baker, C.R. (1979). Defining and measuring affiliation motivation. ''European Journal of Social Psychology'', ''9'', 97-99.</ref>

== In childhood ==
Peer rejection has been measured using [[sociometry]] and other rating methods. Studies typically show that some children are popular, receiving generally high ratings, many children are in the middle, with moderate ratings, and a minority of children are rejected, showing generally low ratings. One measure of rejection asks children to list peers they like and dislike. Rejected children receive few "like" nominations and many "dislike" nominations. Children classified as ''neglected'' receive few nominations of either type.{{citation needed|date=May 2013}}

According to Karen Bierman of Pennsylvania State University, most children who are rejected by their peers display one or more of the following behavior patterns:
*Low rates of [[altruism|prosocial]] behavior, e.g. taking turns, sharing
*High rates of [[aggression|aggressive]] or disruptive behavior
*High rates of inattentive, immature, or [[Impulsivity|impulsive]] behavior
*High rates of [[social anxiety]]

Bierman states that well-liked children show [[social skills|social savvy]] and know when and how to join play groups. Children who are at risk for rejection are more likely to barge in disruptively, or hang back without joining at all. Aggressive children who are athletic or have good social skills are likely to be accepted by peers, and they may become ringleaders in the harassment of less skilled children. Minority children, children with disabilities, or children who have unusual characteristics or behavior may face greater risks of rejection. Depending on the norms of the peer group, sometimes even minor differences among children lead to rejection or neglect. Children who are less outgoing or simply prefer solitary play are less likely to be rejected than children who are socially inhibited and show signs of insecurity or anxiety.<ref name="Bierman, K. L. 2003">Bierman, K. L. (2003). ''Peer rejection: Developmental processes and intervention strategies''. New York: The Guilford Press.</ref>

[[File:Bullying on Instituto Regional Federico Errázuriz (IRFE) in March 5, 2007.jpg|thumb|right|Rejected children are more likely to be bullied at school and on playgrounds{{citation needed|date=May 2013}}]]

Peer rejection, once established, tends to be stable over time, and thus is difficult for a child to overcome.<ref>Cillessen, A., Bukowski, W. M., & Haselager, G. (2000). ''Stability of sociometric categories''. San Francisco, CA: Jossey-Bass.</ref> Researchers have found that active rejection is more stable, more harmful, and more likely to persist after a child transfers to another school, than simple neglect.<ref name="Bierman, K. L. 2003"/> One reason for this is that peer groups establish reputational biases that act as [[stereotype]]s and influence subsequent social interaction.<ref>Hymel, S., Wagner, E., & Butler, L. J. (1990). Reputational bias: View from the peer group. In S. R. Asher, & J. D. Coie, (Eds.). ''Peer rejection in childhood''. Cambridge University Press.</ref> Thus, even when rejected and popular children show similar behavior and accomplishments, popular children are treated much more favorably.

Rejected children are likely to have lower [[self-esteem]], and to be at greater risk for ''internalizing'' problems like depression.<ref name="McDougall, P. 2001 pp. 213-247"/> Some rejected children display ''externalizing'' behavior and show [[aggression]] rather than depression. The research is largely correlational, but there is evidence of reciprocal effects. This means that children with problems are more likely to be rejected, and this rejection then leads to even greater problems for them. Chronic peer rejection may lead to a negative developmental cycle that worsens with time.<ref>Coie, J. D. (1990). Toward a theory of peer rejection. In S. R. Asher & J. D. Coie (Eds). ''Peer rejection in childhood'' (pp. 365–401). Cambridge, England: Cambridge University Press.</ref>

Rejected children are more likely to be [[bullying|bullied]] and to have fewer friends than popular children, but these conditions are not always present. For example, some popular children do not have close friends, whereas some rejected children do. Peer rejection is believed to be less damaging for children with at least one close friend.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Mrug |first1=Sylvie |last2=Molina |first2=Brooke S. G. |last3=Hoza |first3=Betsy |last4=Gerdes |first4=Alyson C. |last5=Hinshaw |first5=Stephen P. |last6=Hechtman |first6=Lily |last7=Arnold |first7=L. Eugene |date=August 2012 |title=Peer Rejection and Friendships in Children with Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder: Contributions to Long-Term Outcomes |journal=Journal of Abnormal Child Psychology |volume=40 |issue=6 |pages=1013–1026 |doi=10.1007/s10802-012-9610-2 |pmid=22331455 |pmc=3384771 }}</ref>

An analysis of 15 [[school shootings]] between 1995 and 2001 found that peer rejection was present in all but two of the cases (87%). The documented rejection experiences included both acute and chronic rejection and frequently took the form of ostracism, bullying, and romantic rejection. The authors stated that although it is likely that the rejection experiences contributed to the school shootings, other factors were also present, such as depression, poor impulse control, and other psychopathology.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Leary | first1 = M. R. | last2 = Kowalski | first2 = R. M. | last3 = Smith | first3 = L. | year = 2003 | title = Teasing, rejection, and violence: Case studies of the school shootings | journal = Aggressive Behavior | volume = 29 | issue = 3| pages = 202–214 | doi=10.1002/ab.10061}}</ref>

There are programs available for helping children who suffer from social rejection. One large scale review of 79 controlled studies found that social skills training is very effective (''r'' = 0.40 effect size), with a 70% success rate, compared to 30% success in control groups. There was a decline in effectiveness over time, however, with follow-up studies showing a somewhat smaller effect size (''r'' = 0.35).<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Schneider | first1 = B. H. | year = 1992 | title = Didactic methods for enhancing children's peer relations: A quantitative review | journal = Clinical Psychology Review | volume = 12 | issue = 3| pages = 363–382 | doi=10.1016/0272-7358(92)90142-u}}</ref>

== In the laboratory ==
Laboratory research has found that even short-term rejection from strangers can have powerful (if temporary) effects on an individual. In several social psychology [[experiment]]s, people chosen at random to receive messages of social exclusion became more aggressive, more willing to cheat, less willing to help others, and more likely to pursue short-term over long-term goals. Rejection appears to lead very rapidly to self-defeating and antisocial behavior.<ref name="pmid12219857">{{cite journal|vauthors=Twenge JM, Catanese KR, Baumeister RF | title=Social exclusion causes self-defeating behavior | journal=J Pers Soc Psychol | year= 2002 | volume= 83 | issue= 3 | pages= 606–15 | pmid=12219857 | doi= 10.1037/0022-3514.83.3.606| s2cid=197655896 }}</ref>

Researchers have also investigated how the brain responds to social rejection. One study found that the dorsal [[anterior cingulate cortex]] is active when people are experiencing both physical pain and "social pain", in response to social rejection.<ref name="pmid14551436">{{cite journal|vauthors=Eisenberger NI, Lieberman MD, Williams KD | title=Does rejection hurt? An FMRI study of social exclusion | journal=Science | year= 2003 | volume= 302 | issue= 5643 | pages= 290–2 | doi=10.1126/science.1089134 | pmid=14551436  | bibcode=2003Sci...302..290E | s2cid=21253445 }}</ref> A subsequent experiment, also using [[MRI|fMRI]] neuroimaging, found that three regions become active when people are exposed to images depicting rejection themes. These areas are the posterior [[cingulate cortex]], the [[parahippocampal gyrus]], and the dorsal [[anterior cingulate cortex]]. Furthermore, individuals who are high in rejection sensitivity (see below) show less activity in the left [[prefrontal cortex]] and the right dorsal [[superior frontal gyrus]], which may indicate less ability to regulate emotional responses to rejection.<ref name="pmid17536965">{{cite journal|vauthors=Kross E, Egner T, Ochsner K, Hirsch J, Downey G | title=Neural dynamics of rejection sensitivity | journal=J Cogn Neurosci | year= 2007 | volume= 19 | issue= 6 | pages= 945–56 | pmid=17536965 | doi=10.1162/jocn.2007.19.6.945 | citeseerx=10.1.1.386.718 | s2cid=8329246 }}</ref>

An experiment performed in 2007 at the University of California at Berkeley found that individuals with a combination of low [[self-esteem]] and low attentional control are more likely to exhibit eye-blink [[startle response]]s while viewing rejection themed images.<ref name="pmid17894606">{{cite journal|vauthors=Gyurak A, Ayduk O | title=Defensive physiological reactions to rejection: the effect of self-esteem and attentional control on startle responses | journal=Psychol Sci | year= 2007 | volume= 18 | issue= 10 | pages= 886–92 | doi=10.1111/j.1467-9280.2007.01996.x | pmc= 4175371| pmid=17894606  }}</ref> These findings indicate that people who feel bad about themselves are especially vulnerable to rejection, but that people can also control and regulate their emotional reactions.

A study at Miami University indicated that individuals who recently experienced social rejection were better than both accepted and control participants in their ability to discriminate between genuine and fake smiles. Though both accepted and control participants were better than chance (they did not differ from each other), rejected participants were much better at this task, nearing 80% accuracy.<ref name="pmid19000206">{{cite journal|vauthors=Bernstein MJ, Young SG, Brown CM, Sacco DF, Claypool HM | title=Adaptive responses to social exclusion: social rejection improves detection of real and fake smiles | journal=Psychol Sci | year= 2008 | volume= 19 | issue= 10 | pages= 981–3 | doi=10.1111/j.1467-9280.2008.02187.x | pmid=19000206  | s2cid=28499849 }}</ref> This study is noteworthy in that it is one of the few cases of a positive or adaptive consequence of social rejection.

=== Ball toss / cyberball experiments ===
A common experimental technique is the "ball toss" paradigm, which was developed by Kipling Williams and his colleagues at Purdue University.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Williams | first1 = K. D. | last2 = Sommer | first2 = K. L. | year = 1997 | title = Social ostracism by one's coworkers: Does rejection lead to loafing or compensation? | journal = Personality and Social Psychology Bulletin | volume = 23 | issue = 7| pages = 693–706 | doi=10.1177/0146167297237003| s2cid = 145458325 }}</ref> This procedure involves a group of three people tossing a ball back and forth. Unbeknownst to the actual participant, two members of the group are working for the experimenter and following a pre-arranged script. In a typical experiment, half of the subjects will be excluded from the activity after a few tosses and never get the ball again. Only a few minutes of this treatment are sufficient to produce negative [[emotion]]s in the target, including anger and sadness. This effect occurs regardless of self-esteem and other personality differences.

[[Gender differences]] have been found in these experiments. In one study, women showed greater nonverbal engagement whereas men disengaged faster and showed face-saving techniques, such as pretending to be uninterested. The researchers concluded that women seek to regain a sense of belonging whereas men are more interested in regaining self-esteem.<ref>Williams, K. D. & Zadro, L. (2001). Ostracism. In M. R. Leary (Ed.), ''Interpersonal rejection''. (pp. 21-53). New York, NY: Oxford University Press.</ref>

A computerized version of the task known as "cyberball" has also been developed and leads to similar results.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Williams |first1=Kipling D. |last2=Jarvis |first2=Blair |date=February 2006 |title=Cyberball: A program for use in research on interpersonal ostracism and acceptance |journal=Behavior Research Methods |volume=38 |issue=1 |pages=174–180 |doi=10.3758/bf03192765 |pmid=16817529 |issn=1554-351X|doi-access=free }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|last=Zadro|first=Lisa|author2=Williams, Kipling D |author3=Richardson, Rick |title=How low can you go? Ostracism by a computer is sufficient to lower self-reported levels of belonging, control, self-esteem, and meaningful existence|journal=Journal of Experimental Social Psychology|date=30 June 2004|volume=40|issue=4|pages=560–567|doi=10.1016/j.jesp.2003.11.006}}</ref>  Cyberball is a virtual ball toss game where the participant is led to believe they are playing with two other participants sitting at computers elsewhere who can toss the ball to either player. The participant is included in the game for the first few minutes, but then excluded by the other players for the remaining three minutes. A significant advantage of the cyberball software is its openness; Williams made the software available to all researchers. In the software, the researcher can adjust the order of throwing the balls, the user's avatar, the background, the availability of chat, the introductory message and much other information. In addition, researchers can obtain the program's latest version by visiting the official website of CYBERBALL 5.0.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Kipling D. Williams |url=http://www1.psych.purdue.edu/~willia55/Announce/cyberball.htm |access-date=2023-03-28 |website=www1.psych.purdue.edu}}</ref>

This simple and short time period of ostracism has been found to produce significant increases to self-reported levels of anger and sadness, as well as lowering levels of the four needs.  These effects have been found even when the participant is ostracised by out-group members,<ref>{{cite journal|last=Smith|first=A|author2=Williams, K |title=R U There? Effects of ostracism by cell phone messages|journal=Group Dynamics: Theory, Research, and Practice|year=2004|volume=8|issue=4|pages=291–301|doi=10.1037/1089-2699.8.4.291|citeseerx=10.1.1.463.6556}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|last=Williams|first=Kipling|author2=Cheung, C. |author3=Choi, W. |title=CyberOstracism: Effects of being ignored over the internet|journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology|year=2000|volume=79|issue=5|pages=748–762|doi=10.1037/0022-3514.79.5.748|pmid=11079239|s2cid=27409048 }}</ref> when the out-group member is identified as a despised person such as someone in the [[Ku Klux Klan]],<ref>{{cite journal|last=Gonsalkorale|first=K|author2=Williams, K |title=The KKK won't let play: Ostracism even by a despised outgroup hurts|journal=European Journal of Social Psychology|year=2007|volume=37|issue=6|pages=1176–1186|doi=10.1002/ejsp.392|doi-access=free}}</ref> when they know the source of the ostracism is just a computer,<ref>{{cite journal|last=Zadro|first=Lisa|author2=Williams, K |author3=Richardson, R |title=How low can you go? Ostracism by a computer is sufficient to lower self-reported levels of belonging, control, self-esteem and meaningful existence|journal=Journal of Experimental Social Psychology|year=2004|volume=40|issue=4|pages=560–567|doi=10.1016/j.jesp.2003.11.006}}</ref> and even when being ostracised means they will be financially rewarded and being included would incur a financial cost.<ref>{{cite journal|last=Van Beest|first=Ilja|author2=Williams, K |title=When inclusion costs and ostracism pays, ostracism still hurts|journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology|volume=42|issue=2|pages=213–220|doi=10.1016/j.jesp.2005.03.005 |year=2006}}</ref> People feel rejected even when they know they are playing only against the computer. A recent set of experiments using cyberball demonstrated that rejection impairs willpower or self-regulation. Specifically, people who are rejected are more likely to eat cookies and less likely to drink an unpleasant tasting beverage that they are told is good for them. These experiments also showed that the negative effects of rejection last longer in individuals who are high in [[social anxiety]].<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Oaten | first1 = M. | last2 = Williams | first2 = K. D. | last3 = Jones | first3 = A. | last4 = Zadro | first4 = L. | year = 2008 | title = The effects of ostracism on self-regulation in the socially anxious | journal = Journal of Social and Clinical Psychology | volume = 27 | issue = 5| pages = 471–504| doi=10.1521/jscp.2008.27.5.471}}</ref>

=== Life-alone paradigm ===
Another mainstream research method is the "life alone paradigm", which was first developed by Twenge and other scholars<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Twenge |first1=Jean M. |last2=Baumeister |first2=Roy F. |last3=Tice |first3=Dianne M. |last4=Stucke |first4=Tanja S. |date=December 2001 |title=If you can't join them, beat them: Effects of social exclusion on aggressive behavior. |url=http://doi.apa.org/getdoi.cfm?doi=10.1037/0022-3514.81.6.1058 |journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology |language=en |volume=81 |issue=6 |pages=1058–1069 |doi=10.1037/0022-3514.81.6.1058 |pmid=11761307 |issn=1939-1315|url-access=subscription }}</ref> to evoke feelings of rejection by informing subjects of false test results. In contrast to ball toss and cyberball, it focuses on future rejection, i.e. the experience of rejection that participants may potentially experience in the future. Specifically, at the beginning of the experiment, participants complete a personality scale (in the original method, the [[Eysenck Personality Questionnaire]]). They are then informed of their results based on their experimental group rather than the real results. Participants in the "rejected" group are told that their test results indicate that they will be alone in the future, regardless of their current state of life. Participants in the "accepted" group will be told they will have a fulfilling relationship. In the control group, participants are told they would encounter some accidents. In this way, the participants' sense of rejection is awakened to take the subsequent measurement. After the experiment, the researcher will explain the results to the participants and apologise.

Scholars point out that this method may cause more harm to the subjects. For example, the participants will likely experience a more severe effect on executive functioning during the test.<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://www.taylorfrancis.com/books/edit/10.4324/9781315308470/ostracism-exclusion-rejection-kipling-williams-steve-nida |title=Ostracism, Exclusion, and Rejection |date=2016 |publisher=Routledge |editor-last=Williams |editor-first=Kipling D. |doi=10.4324/9781315308470 |isbn=978-1-315-30847-0 |editor-last2=Nida |editor-first2=Steve A.}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Gerber |first1=Jonathan |last2=Wheeler |first2=Ladd |date=September 2009 |title=On Being Rejected: A Meta-Analysis of Experimental Research on Rejection |url=http://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1111/j.1745-6924.2009.01158.x |journal=Perspectives on Psychological Science |language=en |volume=4 |issue=5 |pages=468–488 |doi=10.1111/j.1745-6924.2009.01158.x |pmid=26162220 |s2cid=30969812 |issn=1745-6916|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Therefore, this method faces more significant issue with [[research ethics]] and harms than other rejection experiments. Consequently, researchers use this test with caution in experiments and pay attention to the subjects' reactions afterwards.

== Psychology of ostracism ==
Most of the research on the psychology of [[ostracism]] has been conducted by the social psychologist [[Kipling Williams]].  He and his colleagues have devised a model of ostracism which provides a framework to show the complexity in the varieties of ostracism and the processes of its effects.  There he theorises that ostracism can potentially be so harmful that humans have evolved an efficient warning system to immediately detect and respond to it.<ref name="Williams 2002">{{cite book|last=Williams|first=Kipling D.|title=Ostracism: the power of silence|year=2002|publisher=Guilford|location=New York|isbn=978-1-57230-831-2}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|editor-last=Leary|editor-first=Mark R.|title=Interpersonal rejection|year=2007|publisher=Oxford University Press|location=New York|isbn=978-0-19-513015-7|pages=21–54}}</ref>

In the animal kingdom as well as in primitive human societies, ostracism can lead to death due to the lack of protection benefits and access to sufficient food resources from the group.<ref>{{cite journal|last=Gruter|first=M|author2=Masters, R |title=Ostracism as a social and biological phenomenon: An introduction|journal=Ethology and Sociobiology|year=1986|volume=7|issue=3–4|pages=149–158|doi=10.1016/0162-3095(86)90043-9|s2cid=55712844}}</ref>  Living apart from the whole of society also means not having a mate, so being able to detect ostracism would be a highly adaptive response to ensure survival and continuation of the genetic line.

=== Temporal need-threat model ===
The predominant theoretical model of social rejection is the temporal need-threat model proposed by Williams and his colleagues,<ref>{{Citation |last=Williams |first=Kipling D. |title=Chapter 6 Ostracism: A Temporal Need-Threat Model |date=2009-01-01 |url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0065260108004061 |work=Advances in Experimental Social Psychology |volume=41 |pages=275–314 |access-date=2023-03-29 |publisher=Academic Press |language=en |doi=10.1016/s0065-2601(08)00406-1|url-access=subscription }}</ref> in which the process of social exclusion is divided into three stages: ''reflexive'', ''reflective'', and ''resignation''.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last1=Williams |first1=Kipling D. |last2=Nida |first2=Steve A. |date=April 2011 |title=Ostracism: Consequences and Coping |url=http://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/0963721411402480 |journal=Current Directions in Psychological Science |language=en |volume=20 |issue=2 |pages=71–75 |doi=10.1177/0963721411402480 |s2cid=262310176 |issn=0963-7214|url-access=subscription }}</ref> The reflexive stage happens when social rejection first occurs. It is an immediate effect happened on individuals. Then, the reflective stage enters when the individual starts to reflect and cope with social rejection. Finally, if the rejection last for the long term and the individual cannot successfully cope with it, the social rejection would turn to the resignation stage, where the individual is likely to suffer from severe [[Depression (mood)|depression]] and [[Learned helplessness|helplessness]]. 

==== Reflexive stage ====
The reflexive stage is the first stage of social rejection and refers to the period immediately after social exclusion has occurred. During this stage, Williams proposed that ostracism uniquely poses a threat to four [[fundamental human needs]];<ref name=":0" /> the need to belong, the need for control in social situations, the need to maintain high levels of self-esteem, and the need to have a sense of a meaningful existence.<ref name="Williams 2002" /> When social rejection is related to the individual's social relationships, the individual's need for belonging and self-esteem is threatened; when it is not associated with it, it is primarily a threat to a sense of control and meaningful existence.

Another challenge that individuals need to face at this stage is the sense of pain. Previous scholars have used neurobiological methods to find that social exclusion, whether intentional or unintentional, evokes pain in individuals.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal |last=Williams |first=Kipling D. |date=2007-01-01 |title=Ostracism |url=https://www.annualreviews.org/doi/10.1146/annurev.psych.58.110405.085641 |journal=Annual Review of Psychology |language=en |volume=58 |issue=1 |pages=425–452 |doi=10.1146/annurev.psych.58.110405.085641 |pmid=16968209 |issn=0066-4308|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Specifically, neurobiological evidence suggests that social exclusion increases the [[Anterior cingulate cortex|dorsal anterior cingulate cortex (dACC)]] activation.<ref name=":2">{{Cite journal |last1=Eisenberger |first1=Naomi I. |last2=Lieberman |first2=Matthew D. |last3=Williams |first3=Kipling D. |date=2003-10-10 |title=Does Rejection Hurt? An fMRI Study of Social Exclusion |url=https://www.science.org/doi/10.1126/science.1089134 |journal=Science |language=en |volume=302 |issue=5643 |pages=290–292 |doi=10.1126/science.1089134 |pmid=14551436 |bibcode=2003Sci...302..290E |s2cid=21253445 |issn=0036-8075|url-access=subscription }}</ref> This brain region, in turn, is associated with physiological pain in individuals. Notably, the [[Prefrontal cortex|right ventral prefrontal cortex (RVPFC)]] is also further activated when individuals find that social rejection is intentional;<ref name=":2" /> this brain region is associated with the regulation of pain perception, implying that pain perception decreases when individuals understand the source of this social rejection. Further research suggests that personal traits or environmental factors do not affect this pain.<ref name=":1" />

Thus, people are motivated to remove this pain with behaviors aimed at reducing the likelihood of others ostracising them any further and increasing their inclusionary status.

==== Reflective stage ====
In the reflective stage, individuals begin to think about and try to cope with social rejection. In the need-threat model, their response is referred to as need fortification, i.e. the creation of interventions that respond to the needs they are threatened by in the reflective stage. Specifically, when individuals' self-esteem and sense of belonging are threatened, they will try to integrate more into the group. As a result, these rejected individuals develop more [[Prosocial behavior|pro-social behaviors]], such as helping others and giving gifts. In contrast, when their sense of control and meaning is threatened, they show more [[Anti-social behaviour|antisocial behavior]], such as verbal abuse, fighting, etc., to prove they are essential.<ref name=":1" />

==== Resignation stage ====
When individuals have been in social rejection for a long time and cannot improve their situation through effective coping, they move to the third stage, resignation, in which they do not try to change the problem they are facing but choose to accept it. In Zadro's interview study, in which she interviewed 28 respondents in a state of chronic rejection, she found that the respondents were depressed, self-deprecating and helpless.<ref>{{Cite thesis |title=Ostracism: Empirical studies inspired by real-world experiences of silence and exclusion |url=http://hdl.handle.net/1959.4/54588 |publisher=UNSW Sydney |date=2004 |degree=Thesis |language=English |first=Lisa |last=Zadro|hdl=1959.4/54588 }}</ref><ref name=":1" /> This social rejection can significantly impact the physical and psychological health of the individual.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Williams |first1=Kipling D. |last2=Nida |first2=Steve A. |date=2022-10-01 |title=Ostracism and social exclusion: Implications for separation, social isolation, and loss |url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352250X22000720 |journal=Current Opinion in Psychology |language=en |volume=47 |article-number=101353 |doi=10.1016/j.copsyc.2022.101353 |pmid=35662059 |s2cid=248763503 |issn=2352-250X|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

==== Controversy ====
The controversy over temporal need-threat model has focused on whether it enhances or reduces people's perception of pain. DeWall and Baumeister's research suggests that individuals experience a reduction in pain after rejection, a phenomenon they refer to as emotional numbness, which contradicts Williams et al.'s theory that social rejection enhances pain perception.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=DeWall |first1=C. Nathan |last2=Baumeister |first2=Roy F. |date=July 2006 |title=Alone but feeling no pain: Effects of social exclusion on physical pain tolerance and pain threshold, affective forecasting, and interpersonal empathy. |journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology |language=en |volume=91 |issue=1 |pages=1–15 |doi=10.1037/0022-3514.91.1.1 |pmid=16834476 |issn=1939-1315}}</ref> In this regard, Williams suggests that this phenomenon is likely due to differences in the paradigm used in the study, as when using a long-term paradigm such as Life-Alone, individuals do not feel the possibility of rejoining the group, thus creating emotional numbness.<ref name=":1" /> This is further supported by Bernstein and Claypool, who found that in separate cyberball and life-alone experiments, stronger stimuli of rejection, such as life-alone, protected people through emotional numbness. In contrast, in the case of minor rejection, such as that in cyberball, the individual's system detects the rejection cue and draws attention to it through a sense of pain.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Bernstein |first1=Michael J. |last2=Claypool |first2=Heather M. |date=February 2012 |title=Social Exclusion and Pain Sensitivity: Why Exclusion Sometimes Hurts and Sometimes Numbs |url=http://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/0146167211422449 |journal=Personality and Social Psychology Bulletin |language=en |volume=38 |issue=2 |pages=185–196 |doi=10.1177/0146167211422449 |pmid=21885860 |s2cid=10648152 |issn=0146-1672|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

=== Popularity resurgence ===
There has been recent research into the function of popularity on development, specifically how a transition from ostracization to popularity can potentially reverse the deleterious effects of being socially ostracized. While various theories have been put forth regarding what skills or attributes confer an advantage at obtaining popularity, it appears that individuals who were once popular and subsequently experienced a transient ostracization are often able to employ the same skills that led to their initial popularity to bring about a popularity resurgence.<ref>{{Cite web |title=What Social Behaviors Make People Popular so Quickly? |url=https://www.coursera.org/lecture/popularity/what-social-behaviors-make-people-popular-so-quickly-l0ddb%7D |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211030064102/https://www.coursera.org/lecture/popularity/what-social-behaviors-make-people-popular-so-quickly-l0ddb%7D |archive-date=2021-10-30 |access-date=2022-07-17}}</ref>

== Romantic rejection ==
In contrast to the study of childhood rejection, which primarily examines rejection by a group of peers, some researchers focus on the phenomenon of a single individual rejecting another in the context of a romantic relationship. In both teenagers and adults, ''romantic rejection'' occurs when a person refuses the [[courtship|romantic advances]] of another, ignores/avoids or is repulsed by someone who is romantically interested in them, or [[unilaterally]] ends an existing [[intimate relationship|relationship]]. The state of [[unrequited love]] is a common experience in youth, but mutual love becomes more typical as people get older.<ref name="Baumeister, R. F. 2001 pp. 55-72">Baumeister, R. F. & Dhavale, D. (2001). Two sides of romantic rejection. In M. R. Leary (Ed.), ''Interpersonal rejection''. (pp. 55-72). New York, NY: Oxford University Press.</ref>

Romantic rejection is a painful, [[emotional]] experience that appears to trigger a response in the [[caudate nucleus]] of the brain, and associated [[dopamine]] and [[cortisol]] activity.<ref>Fisher, H. (2006) Lost Love: The Nature of romantic rejection, In Cut Loose: (mostly) midlife and older women on the end of (mostly) long-term relationships. Nan Bauer-Maglin (Ed.) New Jersey: Rutgers University Press.</ref> Subjectively, rejected individuals experience a range of negative emotions, including [[frustration]], intense [[anger]], [[jealousy]], [[hate]], and eventually, [[depression (mood)|despair]] and possible long-term [[major depressive disorder]]. However, there have been cases where individuals go back and forth between depression and anger.

== Rejection sensitivity ==
{{See also|Attention deficit hyperactivity disorder#Rejection sensitive dysphoria}}
[[Karen Horney]] was the first theorist to discuss the phenomenon of ''rejection sensitivity''.<ref>Downey, G. (2008). The disregulating effect of social threat in people with borderline personality disorder. Paper presented at the Annual Meeting of the Society for Personality and Social Psychology, February 7–9, 2008.</ref> She suggested that it is a component of the [[neuroticism|neurotic]] personality, and that it is a tendency to feel deep anxiety and humiliation at the slightest rebuff. Simply being made to wait, for example, could be viewed as a rejection and met with extreme anger and hostility.<ref>Horney, K. (1937). ''The neurotic personality of our time''. New York: W. W. Norton and Company.</ref>

[[Albert Mehrabian]] developed an early questionnaire measure of rejection sensitivity.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Mehrabian | first1 = A | year = 1976 | title = Questionnaire measures of affiliative tendency and sensitivity to rejection | journal = Psychological Reports | volume = 38 | pages = 199–209 | doi=10.2466/pr0.1976.38.1.199| s2cid = 144417099 }}</ref> Mehrabian suggested that sensitive individuals are reluctant to express opinions, tend to avoid arguments or controversial discussions, are reluctant to make requests or impose on others, are easily hurt by negative feedback from others, and tend to rely too much on familiar others and situations so as to avoid rejection.

A more recent (1996) definition of rejection sensitivity is the tendency to "anxiously expect, readily perceive, and overreact" to social rejection.<ref name="pmid8667172">{{cite journal|vauthors=Downey G, Feldman SI |title=Implications of rejection sensitivity for intimate relationships |journal=J Pers Soc Psychol |url=http://www.ffri.uniri.hr/~ibrdar/komunikacija/seminari/Downey,%201996%20-%20Rejection%20Sensitivity%20for%20intimate%20relationsh.pdf |year=1996 |volume=70 |issue=6 |pages=1327–43 |pmid=8667172 |doi=10.1037/0022-3514.70.6.1327 |s2cid=7822358 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130731044210/http://www.ffri.uniri.hr/~ibrdar/komunikacija/seminari/Downey,%201996%20-%20Rejection%20Sensitivity%20for%20intimate%20relationsh.pdf |archive-date=July 31, 2013 }}</ref> People differ in their readiness to perceive and react to rejection.<ref name="pmid8667172"/> The causes of individual differences in rejection sensitivity are not well understood. Because of the association between rejection sensitivity and neuroticism, there is a likely genetic predisposition.<ref name=Butler>{{cite journal | last1 = Butler | first1 = J. C. | last2 = Doherty | first2 = M. S. | last3 = Potter | first3 = R. M. | year = 2007 | title = Social antecedents and consequences of interpersonal rejection sensitivity | url = http://www.smsu.edu/facultystaff/jamesbutler/rejectsensitivity.pdf | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20140602200751/http://www.smsu.edu/facultystaff/jamesbutler/rejectsensitivity.pdf | archive-date = 2014-06-02 | journal = Personality and Individual Differences | volume = 43 | issue =  6| pages = 1376–1385 | doi = 10.1016/j.paid.2007.04.006 }}</ref> ''Rejection sensitive dysphoria'', while not a formal diagnosis, is also a common symptom of [[attention deficit hyperactivity disorder]], estimated to affect a majority of people with ADHD. Rejection sensitivity is also a defining characteristic of borderline personality disorder (BPD), where it manifests as intense fear of abandonment and extreme emotional reactions to perceived rejection or criticism.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Bedrossian |first=Louise |date=2021-04-18 |title=Understand and address complexities of rejection sensitive dysphoria in students with ADHD |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/dhe.31047 |journal=Disability Compliance for Higher Education |language=en |volume=26 |issue=10 |page=4 |doi=10.1002/dhe.31047 |s2cid=234849318 |issn=1086-1335|url-access=subscription }}</ref><ref>[https://www.additudemag.com/rejection-sensitive-dysphoria-and-adhd/ How ADHD Ignites Rejection Sensitive Dysphoria] at additudemag.com</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Ginapp |first1=Callie M. |last2=Greenberg |first2=Norman R. |last3=MacDonald-Gagnon |first3=Grace |last4=Angarita |first4=Gustavo A. |last5=Bold |first5=Krysten W. |last6=Potenza |first6=Marc N. |date=2023-10-12 |title='Dysregulated not deficit': A qualitative study on symptomatology of ADHD in young adults |journal=PLOS ONE |language=en |volume=18 |issue=10 |article-number=e0292721 |doi=10.1371/journal.pone.0292721 |pmid=37824501 |pmc=10569543 |doi-access=free }}</ref> Others posit that rejection sensitivity stems from early [[Attachment in children|attachment relationships]] and parental rejection;<ref name = Butler/> peer rejection is also thought to play a role.<ref name = Butler/><ref>{{cite journal | last1 = London | first1 = B. | last2 = Downey | first2 = G. | last3 = Bonica | first3 = C. | last4 = Paltin | first4 = I. | year = 2007 | title = Social causes and consequences of rejection sensitivity | journal = Journal of Research on Adolescence | volume = 17 | issue = 3| pages = 481–506 | doi=10.1111/j.1532-7795.2007.00531.x}}</ref> Bullying, an extreme form of peer rejection, is likely connected to later rejection sensitivity.<ref name = Butler/> However, there is no conclusive evidence for any of these theories.<ref name = Butler/>

The Adult Rejection Sensitivity Questionnaire (A-RSQ), developed by Kathy R. Berenson and colleagues in 2009,<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Berenson |first1=Kathy R. |last2=Gyurak |first2=Anett |last3=Ayduk |first3=Özlem |last4=Downey |first4=Geraldine |last5=Garner |first5=Matthew J. |last6=Mogg |first6=Karin |last7=Bradley |first7=Brendan P. |author8-link=Daniel S. Pine |last8=Pine |first8=Daniel S. |date=2009-12-01 |title=Rejection sensitivity and disruption of attention by social threat cues |journal=Journal of Research in Personality |volume=43 |issue=6 |pages=1064–1072 |doi=10.1016/j.jrp.2009.07.007 |issn=0092-6566 |pmc=2771869 |pmid=20160869}}</ref> is a self-report instrument designed to measure an individual's disposition to anxiously expect, readily perceive, and intensely react to rejection. Validation by Innamorati et al. tested various factor models.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Innamorati |first1=Marco |last2=Balsamo |first2=Michela |last3=Fairfield |first3=Beth |last4=Fabbricatore |first4=Mariantonietta |last5=Tamburello |first5=Antonino |last6=Saggino |first6=Aristide |date=2014 |title=Construct Validity and Reliability of the Adult Rejection Sensitivity Questionnaire: A Comparison of Three Factor Models |journal=Depression Research and Treatment |language=en |volume=2014 |pages=1–10 |doi=10.1155/2014/972424 |doi-access=free |issn=2090-1321 |pmc=4109080 |pmid=25110588}}</ref> A 2025 validation in Russia confirmed a two-factor structure (Concern vs. Expectancy).<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Iurina |first1=E. |last2=Kosonogov |first2=V. |date=2025-10-01 |title=Validation of the Russian version of the Adult Rejection Sensitivity Questionnaire (A-RSQ): Factor structure and associations with psychological constructs |url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1876201825003466 |journal=Asian Journal of Psychiatry |volume=112 |article-number=104703 |doi=10.1016/j.ajp.2025.104703 |pmid=40972439 |issn=1876-2018|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Several studies verified clinical significance of the questionnaire; thus, high scores are strongly associated with rumination,<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Pearson |first1=Katherine A. |last2=Watkins |first2=Edward R. |last3=Mullan |first3=Eugene G. |date=2011-10-01 |title=Rejection sensitivity prospectively predicts increased rumination |url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0005796711001161 |journal=Behaviour Research and Therapy |volume=49 |issue=10 |pages=597–605 |doi=10.1016/j.brat.2011.06.004 |pmid=21764037 |issn=0005-7967|url-access=subscription }}</ref> depression,<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Rubeis |first1=Jannika De |last2=Lugo |first2=Ricardo G. |last3=Witthöft |first3=Michael |last4=Sütterlin |first4=Stefan |last5=Pawelzik |first5=Markus R. |last6=Vögele |first6=Claus |date=2017-10-19 |title=Rejection sensitivity as a vulnerability marker for depressive symptom deterioration in men |journal=PLOS ONE |language=en |volume=12 |issue=10 |article-number=e0185802 |doi=10.1371/journal.pone.0185802 |doi-access=free |issn=1932-6203 |pmc=5648166 |pmid=29049292 |bibcode=2017PLoSO..1285802D }}</ref> and borderline personality disorder.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Foxhall |first1=Mia |last2=Hamilton-Giachritsis |first2=Catherine |last3=Button |first3=Katherine |date=September 2019 |title=The link between rejection sensitivity and borderline personality disorder: A systematic review and meta-analysis |url=https://bpspsychub.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/bjc.12216 |journal=British Journal of Clinical Psychology |language=en |volume=58 |issue=3 |pages=289–326 |doi=10.1111/bjc.12216 |pmid=30900278 |issn=0144-6657|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

== Health effects ==
Social rejection has a large effect on a person's [[health]].  [[Roy Baumeister|Baumeister]] and [[Mark Leary|Leary]] originally suggested that an unsatisfied need to belong would inevitably lead to problems in behavior as well as [[mental health|mental]] and physical health.<ref name="pmid7777651" />  Corroboration of these assumptions about behavior deficits were seen by [[John Bowlby]] in his research.<ref name =  "Bowlby, 1969" >Bowlby, J. (1969). Attachment and loss: Vol. 1. Attachment. New York:Basic Books.</ref> Numerous studies have found that being socially rejected leads to  an increase in levels of [[anxiety]].<ref name =  " Barden, Garber, Leiman, Ford, and Masters (1985)" >Barden. R. C, Garber, J., Leiman, B., Ford, M. E., & Masters, J. C. (1985). Factors governing the effective remediation of negative affect
and its cognitive and behavioral consequences. ''Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 49,'' 1040-1053.</ref><ref name =  "Baumeister & Tice, 1990" >{{cite journal | last1 = Baumeister | first1 = R. F. | last2 = Tice | first2 = D. M. | year = 1990 | title = Point-counterpoints: Anxiety and social exclusion | journal = Journal of Social and Clinical Psychology | volume = 9 | issue = 2| pages = 165–195 | doi = 10.1521/jscp.1990.9.2.165 }}</ref><ref name =  "Besser & Priel, 2009" >{{cite journal | last1 = Besser | first1 = A. | last2 = Priel | first2 = B. | year = 2009 | title = Emotional Responses to a Romantic Partner's Imaginary Rejection: The Roles of Attachment Anxiety, Covert Narcissism, and Self-Evaluation | journal = Journal of Personality | volume = 77 | issue = 1| pages = 287–325 | doi = 10.1111/j.1467-6494.2008.00546.x | pmid = 19076997 }}</ref><ref name =  "Tambor & Leary, 1993" >Tambor, E. S., & Leary, M. R. (1993). Perceived exclusion as a common factor in social anxiety, loneliness, jealousy, depression, and low self-esteem. Unpublished manuscript. Winston-Salem, NC: Wake Forest University.</ref> Additionally, the level of [[depression (mood)|depression]] a person feels as well as the amount they care about their social relationships is directly proportional to the level of rejection they perceive.<ref name = "Tambor & Leary, 1993" />  Rejection affects the emotional health and well being of a person as well. Overall, experiments show that those who have been rejected will suffer from more negative emotions and have fewer positive emotions than those who have been accepted or those who were in neutral or control conditions.<ref name =  "Blackhart, Nelson, Knowles, & Baumeister (2009)">{{cite journal | last1 = Blackhart | first1 = G. C. | last2 = Nelson | first2 = B. C. | last3 = Knowles | first3 = M. L. | last4 = Baumeister | first4 = R. F. | year = 2009 | title = Rejection elicits emotional reactions but neither causes immediate distress nor lowers self-esteem: A meta-analytic review of 192 studies on social exclusion | journal = Personality and Social Psychology Review | volume = 13 | issue = 4| pages = 269–309 | doi = 10.1177/1088868309346065 | pmid = 19770347 | s2cid = 15945219 }}</ref>

In addition to the emotional response to rejection, there is a large effect on physical health as well. Having poor relationships and being more frequently rejected is predictive of mortality.<ref name =  "Orth-Gomer & Johnson, 1987" >{{cite journal | last1 = Orth-Gomer | first1 = K. | last2 = Johnson | first2 = J. V. | year = 1987 | title = Social network interaction and mortality: a six year follow-up study of a random sample of the Swedish population | journal = Journal of Chronic Diseases | volume = 40 | issue = 10| pages = 949–957 | doi=10.1016/0021-9681(87)90145-7| pmid = 3611293 }}</ref> Also, as long as a decade after a marriage ends, divorced women have higher rates of illness than their non-married or currently married counterparts.<ref name =  "Lorenz & Wickrama, 2006" >{{cite journal | last1 = Lorenz | first1 = F. O. | last2 = Wickrama | first2 = K. A. S. | last3 = Conger | first3 = R. D. | last4 = Elder | first4 = G. H. | year = 2006 | title = The short-term and decade-long effects of divorce on women's midlife health | journal = Journal of Health and Social Behavior | volume = 47 | issue = 2| pages = 111–125 | doi=10.1177/002214650604700202| pmid = 16821506 | s2cid = 6918975 }}</ref> In the case of a [[family estrangement]], a core part of the mother's identity may be betrayed by the rejection of an adult child.<ref>Agllias, K. (2011a). Every family: Intergenerational estrangement between older parents and their adult-children. (Doctoral dissertation, School of Humanities and Social Sciences, University of Newcastle, Callaghan).</ref> The chance for reconciliation, however slight, results in an inability to attain closure.<ref>Agllias, Kylie. (Sep 2013). Family Estrangement. Encyclopedia of Social Work. Subject: Couples and Families, Aging and Older Adults, Children and Adolescents. {{doi|10.1093/acrefore/9780199975839.013.919}}</ref>  The resulting emotional state and societal stigma from the estrangement may harm the psychological and physical health of the parent for the rest of their life.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Bowling | first1 = A. | last2 = Gabriel | first2 = Z. | year = 2007 | title = Lay theories of quality of life in older age | url = http://discovery.ucl.ac.uk/176071/| journal = Ageing and Society | volume = 27 | issue = 6| pages = 827–848 | doi=10.1017/s0144686x07006423| s2cid = 16940454 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Gabriel | first1 = Z. | last2 = Bowling | first2 = A. | year = 2004 | title = Quality of life from the perspectives of older people | url = http://discovery.ucl.ac.uk/1648/| journal = Ageing and Society | volume = 24 | issue = 5| pages = 675–691 | doi=10.1017/s0144686x03001582| s2cid = 145106062 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | last1 = MacDonald | first1 = M | year = 2007 | title = Social support for centenarians' health, psychological well-being, and longevity | journal = Annual Review of Gerontology and Geriatrics | volume = 27 | pages = 107–127 }}</ref>

The [[immune system]] tends to be harmed when a person experiences social rejection.<ref name =  "Dickerson, Grunewald, & Kemeny (2004)" >{{cite journal | last1 = Dickerson | first1 = S. S. | last2 = Gruenewald | first2 = T. L. | last3 = Kemeny | first3 = M. E. | year = 2004 | title = When the social self is threatened: Shame, physiology, and health | journal = Journal of Personality | volume = 72 | issue = 6| pages = 1191–1216 | doi=10.1111/j.1467-6494.2004.00295.x| pmid = 15509281 }}</ref><ref name =  "Cole, Kemeny, & Taylor (1997)">{{cite journal | last1 = Cole | first1 = S. W. | last2 = Kemeny | first2 = M. E. | last3 = Taylor | first3 = S. E. | year = 1997 | title = Social identity and physical health: Accelerated HIV progression in rejection-sensitive gay men | journal = Journal of Personality and Social Psychology | volume = 72 | issue = 2| pages = 320–335 | doi=10.1037/0022-3514.72.2.320| pmid = 9107003 }}</ref> This can cause severe problems for those with diseases such as [[HIV]]. One study by Cole, Kemeny, and [[Shelley E. Taylor|Taylor]] investigated the differences in the disease progression of HIV-positive gay men who were sensitive to rejection compared to those who were not considered rejection sensitive.<ref name =  "Cole, Kemeny, & Taylor (1997)" />  The study, which took place over nine years, indicated significantly faster rates of low [[T helper cells]], therefore leading to an earlier [[AIDS]] diagnosis. They also found that those patients who were more sensitive to rejection died  from the disease an average of two years earlier than their non-rejection sensitive counterparts.<ref name =  "Cole, Kemeny, & Taylor (1997)" />

Other aspects of health are also affected by rejection. Both [[systolic blood pressure|systolic]] and [[diastolic blood pressure]] increase upon imagining a rejection scenario.<ref name =  "Sommer et al., 2009">{{cite journal | last1 = Sommer | first1 = K. L. | last2 = Kirkland | first2 = K. L. | last3 = Newman | first3 = S. R. | last4 = Estrella | first4 = P. | last5 = Andreassi | first5 = J. L. | year = 2009 | title = Narcissism and cardiovascular reactivity to rejection imagery | journal = Journal of Applied Social Psychology | volume = 39 | issue = 5| pages = 1083–1115 | doi = 10.1111/j.1559-1816.2009.00473.x }}</ref> Those who are socially rejected have an increased likelihood of suffering from [[tuberculosis]], as well as [[suicide]].<ref name =  "Cassel, 1976">{{cite journal | last1 = Cassel | first1 = J | year = 1976 | title = The contribution of the social environment to host resistance | journal = American Journal of Epidemiology | volume = 104 | issue = 2| pages = 107–123 | doi = 10.1093/oxfordjournals.aje.a112281 | pmid = 782233 }}</ref> Rejection and isolation were found to affect levels of pain following an operation<ref name =  "Lidderdale & Walsh, 1998">{{cite journal | last1 = Lidderdale | first1 = J. M. | last2 = Walsh | first2 = J. J. | year = 1998 | title = The effects of social support on cardiovascular reactivity and perinatal outcome | journal = Psychology and Health | volume = 13 | issue = 6| pages = 1061–1070 | doi=10.1080/08870449808407450}}</ref> as well as other physical forms of pain.<ref name =  "Besser & Priel, 2009" /> MacDonald and [[Mark Leary|Leary]] theorize that rejection and exclusion cause physical pain because that pain is a warning sign to support human survival. As humans developed into social creatures, social interactions and relationships became necessary for survival, and the physical pain systems already existed within the human body.<ref name =  "MacDonald & Leary, 2005">{{cite journal | last1 = MacDonald | first1 = G. | last2 = Leary | first2 = M. R. | year = 2005 | title = Why does social exclusion hurt? The relationship between social and physical pain | journal = Psychological Bulletin | volume = 131 | issue = 2| pages = 202–23 | doi = 10.1037/0033-2909.131.2.202 | pmid=15740417| citeseerx = 10.1.1.335.4272 | s2cid = 10540325 }}</ref>

== In fiction, film and art ==
[[File:William Frith - Pope Makes Love To Lady Mary Wortley Montagu - Google Art Project.jpg|thumb|The painting ''Pope Makes Love To Lady Mary Wortley Montagu'' by [[William Powell Frith]] depicts [[Lady Mary Wortley Montagu]] laughingly rejecting [[Alexander Pope]]'s courtship]]
Artistic depictions of rejection occur in a variety of art forms.  One genre of film that most frequently depicts rejection is romantic comedies. In the film ''[[He's Just Not That Into You (film)|He's Just Not That Into You]]'', the main characters deal with the challenges of reading and misreading human behavior. This presents a fear of rejection in romantic relationships as reflected in this quote by the character Mary, "And now you have to go around checking all these different portals just to get rejected by seven different technologies. It's exhausting."<ref>{{IMDb title|qid=Q155559|id=tt1001508|title=He's Just Not That Into You}}</ref>

Social rejection is also depicted in theatrical plays and musicals. For example, the film ''[[Hairspray (1988 film)|Hairspray]]'' shares the story of Tracy Turnblad, an overweight 15-year-old dancer set in the 1960s. Tracy and her mother are faced with overcoming society's expectations regarding weight and physical appearances.

== Further reading ==
*Asher, S. R., & Coie, J. D. (1990). [https://www.amazon.com/dp/0521391830 ''Peer rejection in childhood''.] Cambridge University Press.
*Bierman, K. L. (2003). [https://www.amazon.com/gp/reader/1572309237 ''Peer rejection: Developmental processes and intervention strategies''.] New York: The Guilford Press.
*Leary, M. (2001). [https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0013PL4EM ''Interpersonal rejection''.] New York, NY: Oxford University Press.
*Savage, E. (2002). [https://www.amazon.com/gp/reader/0595255752 ''Don't take it personally: The art of dealing with rejection''.] Oakland, CA: New Harbinger Publications.
*Williams, K. D., Forgás, J. P., & von Hippel, W. (2005). [https://www.amazon.com/gp/reader/184169424X ''The social outcast: Ostracism, social exclusion, rejection, and bullying''.] New York: Psychology Press.
